pages.title: Welcome to Password Basics
pages.subtitle: Learn how strong passwords help protect your accounts, data, and organization.

pages.title: Why passwords matter
pages.text:
Passwords are one of the first lines of defense for protecting systems and information.

If an attacker gains access to a password, they may be able to log in as a legitimate user and access sensitive systems, files, or data.

Many password related security incidents begin with the weakest link, the human factor.

Reference video: YouTube source.

pages.title: Length vs. complexity
pages.text:
Longer passwords are usually better than shorter passwords that only look complicated.

NIST guidance says password length is a primary factor in password strength. It also says systems should not force composition rules like requiring a mix of uppercase letters, lowercase letters, numbers, and symbols.

That is because people often respond to those old rules in predictable ways, such as creating passwords like Password1! or Summer2025!. Those may look complex, but they are still common and guessable.

Simplified comparison:

Example TypeExampleTakeaway
Short + predictablePassword1!Looks complex, but still common and easier to guess.
Short + symbolsn@4gr!D3#Better than a weak password, but short length still limits strength.
Longer passwordBlueFoxRiverTrain7More length usually means better resistance to guessing and cracking.
Long passphraseBlueRiverCoffeeTrainWindowLong passphrases are often easier to remember and much stronger.

That is why the next page focuses on passphrases as a practical way to create passwords that are both stronger and easier to remember.

Reference: NIST SP 800-63B | Hive Systems annual password table infographic

Length and uniqueness matter more than making a short password look complicated.

pages.title: Want to learn more?
pages.text:
The Hive Systems password chart is a widely shared annual infographic that helps visualize how password length and complexity affect cracking difficulty.

Bitwarden also offers a password strength tester that can help show how length, randomness, and uniqueness affect password strength.

Open the Hive Systems annual password table discussion video.

Open the Bitwarden password strength tester.

Bitwarden says the tester processes passwords locally in your browser rather than transmitting them to their servers.

pages.title: Passphrases
pages.text:
A passphrase is a longer password made of multiple words.

Passphrases are often easier to remember than short, random-looking passwords while still being harder for attackers to guess because of their length.

Examples:

ExampleWhy it matters
BlueRiverCoffeeTrainWindowLong, memorable, and harder to guess because of its length.
SunlightBridgePaperPlanetMade of multiple unrelated words, which helps make it stronger.
DogCharlie2012Weaker because it may include personal or guessable information.

Try to avoid using pet names, family names, birthdays, or other personal details that someone might guess or find online.

A good passphrase should be long, memorable, and not based on personal information.

pages.title: Did you know?
pages.text: Password security is not only about making a password look complicated. It is also about making it hard to guess and easy to manage safely.

bullets.title: Longer passwords are often stronger
bullets.text: A passphrase made of several words can be both stronger and easier to remember than a short password with a few symbols added.

bullets.title: Composition rules can backfire
bullets.text: When people are forced to add symbols or numbers, they often make predictable changes that attackers already expect.

bullets.title: Sticky notes create physical risk
bullets.text: A password written near a desk, laptop, or monitor may be exposed to coworkers, visitors, or anyone nearby.

bullets.title: Password managers and MFA reduce human error
bullets.text: These tools help people avoid reuse and add protection even if a password is exposed.

pages.title: Common password mistakes
pages.text:
Many people choose passwords that are easy to remember, but some common habits make accounts easier to compromise.

Common mistakeWhy it is risky
Using a pet's name, family name, or favorite team.Personal details may be guessed or found online.
Using birthdays or simple patterns.These are common choices attackers often try first.
Reusing the same password across multiple websites.One breached account can put many other accounts at risk.
Writing passwords on sticky notes or storing them in plain text.Anyone nearby may see them, and they can be easily exposed.

These habits are understandable, but attackers often try these patterns first.

Small password habits can create big security risks.

pages.title: Password reuse risks
pages.text:
Using the same password across multiple websites is dangerous.

If one website is breached, attackers often try the same password on other services. This technique is called credential stuffing.

A single reused password can put multiple accounts at risk.

pages.title: Better alternatives to sticky notes
pages.text:
Remembering many strong passwords is difficult, which is why insecure shortcuts are common.

Safer alternatives include:

  • Use a password manager to store strong, unique passwords.
  • Use a long passphrase when you must remember a password yourself.
  • Enable multi-factor authentication (MFA) whenever it is available.

Examples of password managers:

Password managerWhy people use itOfficial link
BitwardenStores unlimited passwords, generates strong passwords, and syncs across devices.bitwarden.com
1PasswordSecure vault, autofill, and strong password generation features.1password.com
DashlanePassword storage with security alerts and autofill.dashlane.com
Proton PassPrivacy-focused password storage and autofill.proton.me/pass

Examples of safe authenticator apps (for MFA):

AuthenticatorWhat it doesOfficial link
Google AuthenticatorGenerates time-based one-time codes for login security.Google Authenticator
Microsoft AuthenticatorProvides push approvals, one-time codes, and supports passkeys.Microsoft Authenticator
Bitwarden AuthenticatorGenerates one-time codes and is designed to support MFA across accounts and apps.Bitwarden Authenticator
Duo MobileUsed by organizations for push-based MFA and secure login approvals.duo.com

Password managers and authenticator apps work together: one stores your passwords, and the other adds an extra layer of protection.

Using both a password manager and MFA significantly reduces the risk of account compromise.

pages.title: If you think a password is compromised
pages.text:
If you believe your password may be exposed:

  1. Change the password immediately.
  2. Update any other accounts using the same password.
  3. Enable MFA if it is available and not already turned on.
  4. Report the incident according to your organization's process.

Quick action helps reduce the risk of unauthorized access.
